title: People CARE Act
stage_label: Referred to the Subcommittee on Nutrition and Foreign Agriculture.
plain_summary: This bill creates a special group called a commission to study government programs that help low-income people, like food stamps and Medicaid. The commission will look for ways to make these programs work better together and help people become more independent. They want to fix problems where people lose all their benefits too quickly when they start earning more money. The commission will also create a website where regular people can suggest improvements. When the commission finishes their work, Congress will vote on their ideas using a faster process than usual.
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Nutrition and Foreign Agriculture.
subjects: Energy assistance for the poor and aged
Housing for the elderly and disabled
Social work, volunteer service, charitable organizations

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the People-Centered Assistance Reform Effort Act or the People CARE Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Purpose
sections.text: The goal of this law is to change government help programs so that they work together better. The law wants these programs to help people in a complete way by giving social workers better tools. The programs should be easier to understand and work together smoothly. Over time, people using these programs should be able to earn enough money on their own so they no longer need government help. The programs should help people get jobs, build healthy families, and get more education so households can stay above the poverty level without needing help from the federal government.

sections.title: Section 3 Definition of Means-Tested Welfare Program
sections.text: A means-tested welfare program is any federal program designed to help only low-income Americans. These programs do not include benefits that people earned through work, benefits that are not based on need, benefits for veterans, or programs that most working people and their families can use. The definition does include community programs that help low-income neighborhoods. The law lists many specific programs that count as means-tested welfare programs. These include cash help like Supplemental Security Income and tax credits for low-income families. Medical programs include Medicaid and the Children's Health Insurance Program. Food programs include food stamps and free school meals. Housing programs include rental assistance and public housing. Energy programs help with heating bills and phone service. Education programs include Pell Grants and Title One school funding. Training programs include Job Corps and adult job training. Service programs include social services and legal aid. Child care programs include Head Start. Community development programs help improve poor neighborhoods. The law also lists programs that do not count as means-tested welfare. These include Social Security retirement benefits, Medicare, veterans benefits, unemployment benefits, and workers compensation. Only the refundable parts of certain tax credits count as welfare programs. This means the part that gets paid to people even if they do not owe any taxes. State and local government programs that use their own money and do not get federal grants are not included.

sections.title: Section 4 Establishment of People-Centered Assistance Reform Effort Commission
sections.text: This law creates a new group called the People-Centered Assistance Reform Effort Commission or CARE Commission. The Commission will review all government welfare programs to find ways to make them work better together and cost less money. They will look for programs that can be combined or changed to help people more effectively. The Commission will try to eliminate benefit cliffs, which happen when people lose benefits too quickly as their income goes up. They will find ways to give caseworkers a complete view of all programs a person or family might qualify for. The Commission will also look for ways to save money by having private companies or states run some programs instead of the federal government. Within eighteen months of being fully appointed, the Commission must write a report with their findings and recommendations. They must also write a bill with specific changes to the law. The Commission will have eight members total. The majority and minority leaders of both the Senate and House of Representatives will each appoint two members. People who worked as lobbyists in the past five years cannot be on the Commission. The members will choose their own chairperson. The Commission can hold hearings, request information from government agencies, and issue subpoenas to get the information they need. They will set up a website where the public can send in suggestions. The Commission will end either when their bill becomes law or at the end of the Congress when their bill was introduced.

sections.title: Section 5 Expedited Procedures
sections.text: The Commission's bill must be introduced in both the Senate and House of Representatives within five legislative days after the Commission submits it to Congress. The bill will get special fast-track treatment in both chambers. In the House of Representatives, the bill goes straight to the calendar when introduced. Within ninety legislative days, there can be a motion to consider the bill. The motion cannot be debated and all objections are waived. When the House considers the bill, they get ten hours of debate split equally between supporters and opponents. No amendments are allowed and the bill passes with a simple majority. In the Senate, the bill also goes straight to the calendar. Within ninety legislative days, there can be a motion to consider it. This motion cannot be debated or delayed. Once the Senate starts considering the bill, they get thirty hours of debate split between the majority and minority leaders. No amendments are allowed and the bill passes with a simple majority. If one chamber passes the bill and receives the other chamber's version, they vote on the other chamber's bill instead of their own. If the President vetoes the bill, the Senate gets ten hours to debate overriding the veto. These special rules are part of how each chamber operates but either chamber can change them at any time like any other rule.
